q: What are you currently working on?
a: I'm an AI Solutions Architect and engineering manager at MHP, a Porsche company, in Stuttgart. I build production RAG pipelines, agentic AI workflows, and multi-agent orchestration systems with MCP integrations. My current focus is a multi-agent system serving a large user base, now in client acceptance testing.

q: What is your strongest technical skill?
a: Building production LLM systems end to end — RAG pipelines, agentic workflows, and multi-agent orchestration with MCP integrations like SAP and SharePoint. I pair around three years of hands-on AI/LLM work with roughly ten years of production software engineering, including a deep Java/Spring backend foundation and Python for AI work.

q: Why did you choose your field?
a: I started in backend and data engineering because I liked building systems where correctness and performance both matter. Moving into AI was a natural extension — the same engineering discipline applied to a domain where the systems can reason over knowledge, not just move data. That combination of solid engineering and applied AI is what keeps me in the field.

q: What kind of projects do you enjoy most?
a: Projects where retrieval quality, system performance, and evaluation rigor matter at the same time — for example, comparing retrieval strategies head to head and selecting technology based on measured tradeoffs rather than hype. I prefer building something concrete that demonstrates a result over arguing it in theory.

q: How do you approach learning new technologies?
a: I learn by building. When I need to evaluate something new, I construct a focused project that forces me to use it under realistic constraints, then measure the result. I prefer evidence over claims, and I'm comfortable working as a co-architect — making decisions and iterating forward rather than waiting for full validation at each step.

q: What is your biggest professional achievement?
a: Directing the Azure Cloud migration of AI/ML solutions for more than 20,000 devices, reducing deployment times by 30% and running times by 50%, while maintaining a scalable data lake handling 65 PB+ of data with zero outages across a multinational team.

q: Are you open to new opportunities?
a: Yes. I'm particularly drawn to roles where applied AI meets complex scientific or operational environments, and where the work centers on production RAG, agentic systems, and evaluation frameworks rather than drifting away from that core.

q: What is your preferred tech stack?
a: For open, self-hosted AI work I reach for LlamaIndex, BAAI/bge-m3 embeddings, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct served via Ollama locally or vLLM in production, pgvector with HNSW indexing, RAGAS for evaluation, and LangFuse for tracing. On managed infrastructure I work with Azure OpenAI and the Power Platform. I keep the stack proven and code-first wherever possible.

q: How do you work in a team?
a: Direct and decisive, with a preference for co-architect collaboration and forward momentum. I push back with clear reasoning when something doesn't hold up, and I value working across disciplines — translating between engineers, domain experts, and non-specialists. I've led multiple teams and mentored senior developers on both technical and leadership skills.

q: What domains do you have experience in?
a: Enterprise applied AI, cloud and data engineering, regulated financial systems (central banking), aviation message-handling systems, and large-scale data lake infrastructure. Across these I've worked on production RAG and agentic systems, Azure cloud migration, database and pipeline optimization, and data governance.

q: Tell me about a production AI system you shipped end to end.
a: Mein HR-Assistant, an enterprise HR assistant for a major automotive client, deployed to roughly 16,000 users. The architecture is Copilot Studio with an atomic multi-agent pattern, a custom MCP server on an Azure Web App, and a Co-Driver / DevHub API gateway that does JWT-to-SAML bridging for SAP HCM integration. Retrieval is pgvector hybrid search — BM25 plus dense vectors with reciprocal rank fusion and cross-encoder reranking. I was both developer and tech lead, so I owned the architecture decisions and the implementation, not just the slideware.

q: How do you enforce authorization in a RAG system handling sensitive personnel data?
a: Authorization is enforced at the data layer, never at the LLM. At parse time I tag every chunk with the user groups permitted to see it. At query time I filter by the intersection of the requesting user's groups before anything reaches the model. The LLM only ever sees content the user is already entitled to read. This means a prompt-injection or model error cannot leak unauthorized content, because the filtering happens before retrieval, upstream of the model entirely.

q: How do you think about data sovereignty and the boundary between your tenant and the platform?
a: The decisive line is processor control, not encryption. An operator-controlled Azure tenant inside the EEA is categorically different from a Microsoft-managed SaaS plane, even when both are encrypted, because the question is who is the data processor and under what legal basis. For an organization like EPO operating under the EPC and its own DPR — Article 24 and internal governance, rather than the EU AI Act being directly binding — that distinction drives where you can place an index and which retrieval pattern is admissible. It's the same reasoning whether the data class is personnel records or corporate documents.

q: Microsoft 365 Copilot supports synced and federated connectors. How does that relate to your custom MCP approach?
a: A federated connector retrieves in real time through a protocol like MCP without copying content into the Microsoft-managed Graph semantic index, whereas a synced connector ingests content into that managed index. My custom MCP server is architecturally a federated pattern — retrieval stays in our controlled plane and nothing is copied into a managed index. I built it that way for granular control over retrieval and ranking, and for multimodal data the managed index doesn't handle well. The honest framing is that I arrived at the federated architecture through requirements rather than from the Microsoft connector taxonomy, and I'd want to know that vocabulary cold in an M365-centric environment.

q: How do you make and document architecture decisions?
a: ADR-style trade-off reasoning. I state the decision, the constraints driving it, the options considered, and the risk owner for each path. A concrete example is the MCP server hosting decision — Azure Functions versus Web App versus Container Apps — where I rejected AKS because the operational governance burden outweighed the flexibility for this workload. The principle I keep returning to is that governance shapes architecture: the constraint set usually selects the design before the technology preferences do.

q: How do you evaluate an AI system rather than just demo it?
a: With defined test scenarios and measured retrieval and performance metrics, not anecdote. On the HR assistant I ran a performance test strategy using Locust against the MCP layer and structured Copilot Studio evaluation, and I compared retrieval strategies head to head before committing to the hybrid pgvector approach. I treat evaluation as a gate, not a formality — selecting technology on measured trade-offs is the whole point, and it's what lets me distinguish a capability I've shipped from one I'm only projecting.

q: Why EPO?
a: The mission first — EPO is a European institution where the quality of decisions has real consequences, and AI there has to assist people who stay accountable, not replace their judgement. That fits how I already build: authorization and sovereignty enforced in the architecture, evaluation as a gate, humans deciding. Personally, I hold dual German and Turkish citizenship and have spent my career in European enterprise and regulated environments, so a multinational intergovernmental setting is where I want to contribute. And the technical fit is direct — production RAG, agentic systems, MCP integration, and identity propagation are exactly what I've shipped.

q: EPO's CTO has said culture and change management are harder than the technology. Do you agree?
a: Yes, and my own experience says the same. On the HR assistant the hardest part was not the retrieval architecture — it was stakeholder management across six or seven teams, including Microsoft, each with its own constraints and priorities. The technology decisions were tractable once the organizational alignment was there. I also share the AI-assists-humans-decide stance and the pilot-then-scale instinct: prove a capability under realistic constraints on a contained scope, measure it, then expand, rather than betting an organization on an unproven universal solution.

q: How do you handle vendor dependency and platform risk?
a: I treat it as a real architectural risk, not a procurement footnote. Concretely, I keep portability in the design — pgvector on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server satisfies an Azure-native constraint while keeping the index sovereign and portable, so the organization isn't locked to a single managed service for its core retrieval. I migrated the HR assistant's orchestration from LangGraph to Copilot Studio for enterprise platform standardization but preserved the multi-agent structure, which is the same instinct — align with the platform where it makes sense, but don't surrender the parts that constitute your control or your exit path.

q: What is your educational background?
a: I hold a BSc and an MSc in Computer Engineering from Yıldız Technical University in Istanbul, completed between 2003 and 2011. I also completed the coursework phase of a doctoral programme in Computer Engineering at the same university in 2012, but relocated to Germany before finishing the dissertation. My graduate work gave me a strong foundation in algorithms, systems, and data modelling that I still draw on when designing retrieval architectures and evaluation frameworks.

q: What languages do you speak?
a: Turkish is my native language. I work in English daily — all my professional writing, architecture documentation, and client communication is in English. My German is at B2 level, TELC certified, which lets me navigate German-language work environments comfortably, though I do complex technical discussions in English.

q: What certifications do you hold?
a: DAMA CDMP (data management), TOGAF 9 (enterprise architecture), ISAQB CPSA-F (software architecture), Google Certified Professional Cloud Architect, Azure Solutions Architect (Microsoft), Oracle Certified Java SE 8, ITIL, DevOps Fundamentals, and SAFe for Architects. The ones I use most actively day-to-day are the Azure and data management credentials — they map directly onto the work I do designing and deploying AI systems on Azure with proper data governance.

q: Tell me about your CERN open-stack RAG project.
a: I built a self-hosted retrieval system that benchmarks two retrieval strategies — VectorStoreIndex versus PropertyGraphIndex — over identical CERN public documentation, embeddings, and a generator LLM, so the comparison is fair. The stack is LlamaIndex, BAAI/bge-m3 embeddings, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct served via Ollama locally or vLLM in production, pgvector with HNSW indexing, RAGAS for evaluation, and LangFuse for tracing. The point of the project was to produce a measured, reproducible trade-off comparison rather than an opinion about which retrieval approach is better in theory.

q: How did your career start?
a: I founded a small software company called Young Software in Izmir in 2001 and built hotel management software from scratch as a teenager. After my BSc and MSc I joined the Central Bank of Turkey, where I spent five years building production systems that processed millions of financial data points, developed statistical functions in a strictly regulated environment, and designed data visualisations used by the Governor for international presentations. That early experience in regulated, high-accuracy environments shaped how I think about correctness and auditability — instincts that carry directly into building AI systems where the data and the decision trail need to hold up to scrutiny.

q: What was your work at the Central Bank of Turkey like?
a: Five years building production data systems in a strictly regulated environment. I reduced operational workload by 80% by developing custom statistical functions and correction-control rules, and built the full-stack tools that processed millions of financial data points. I also designed the data visualisations the Governor used for international speeches, which had to be accurate, reliable, and ready on a tight schedule. That experience gave me a discipline around data correctness and governance that I've carried into every role since — it's the same instinct that drives how I handle authorization and auditability in RAG systems today.

q: Where are you located and are you open to relocation?
a: I'm based in Pattonville, near Stuttgart, Baden-Württemberg. I'm open to relocation within Europe for the right role — particularly to The Hague or another major European hub if the mission and technical scope are a strong fit. I'm comfortable with hybrid arrangements and have worked in multinational distributed teams throughout my career.

q: What are you looking for in your next role?
a: A role where applied AI — production RAG, agentic systems, evaluation frameworks — is the core of the work, not a side project. I want to stay close to the architecture and implementation, not drift into pure management. The mission matters too: I'm drawn to institutions and organisations where the AI work has real consequences and requires the kind of rigour I've built up — not just demos, but systems that hold up under scrutiny, handle sensitive data correctly, and assist people who remain accountable for the decisions.
